edges.description: Trauma exposure is required, while inherited liability modifies who develops persistent PTSD symptoms after exposure.
edges.target: Polygenic Susceptibility to PTSD after Trauma

edges.description: Traumatic stress activates stress-response pathways that can become persistently dysregulated in PTSD.
Genetic and environmental susceptibility are represented as upstream of molecular regulation systems involved in stress responses.
edges.source: Polygenic Susceptibility to PTSD after Trauma

edges.description: Epigenetic changes in HPA-axis-related genes are modeled upstream of altered endocrine stress regulation.
Epigenetic changes in immune-function genes are modeled upstream of altered inflammatory signaling.
Stress-hormone regulation interacts with inflammatory signaling in PTSD models and clinical studies.
Stress-axis dysregulation affects limbic and prefrontal systems that regulate fear, anxiety, and memory.
Inflammatory markers are associated with structural and functional alterations in amygdala, hippocampus, and frontal cortex.
Abnormal threat and memory circuitry contributes to intrusive traumatic recollections and flashbacks.
Altered threat circuitry contributes to persistent threat perception, hyperarousal, and anxiety.

nodes.description: Avoidance of trauma-related internal experiences or external reminders.

evidence.reference_title: Epigenetic Alterations in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: Comprehensive Review of Molecular Markers
evidence.snippet: PTSD is characterized by nightmares, flashbacks and avoidance of stressors.

nodes.description: Trauma and other environmental stressors are associated with epigenetic mechanisms affecting HPA-axis, neurotrophic, neurotransmitter, and immune systems relevant to PTSD.

evidence.reference_title: Epigenetic Alterations in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: Comprehensive Review of Molecular Markers
evidence.snippet: Studies show that environmental stressors can induce various epigenetic changes that shape the PTSD phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Epigenetic Alterations in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: Comprehensive Review of Molecular Markers
evidence.snippet: This review investigated gene methylation changes in association with PTSD, including those related to the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, brain-derived neurotrophic factor, neurotransmitters, and immune system functioning, as well as the role of histones and regulatory noncoding RNAs.

nodes.description: PTSD is associated with altered hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis regulation, including lower morning and 24-hour cortisol in meta-analysis, though moderator patterns are inconsistent.

evidence.reference_title: HPA axis regulation in posttraumatic stress disorder: A meta-analysis focusing on potential moderators.
evidence.snippet: Morning and 24 h cortisol were significantly lower in PTSD than in controls (g = -0.21; 95% CI: -0.42-(-0.01); g = -0.31; CI: -0.60-(-0.03)).

nodes.description: Persistent threat perception, heightened arousal, fearfulness, and anxiety.

evidence.reference_title: Assessment and diagnosis of post-traumatic stress disorders (PTSDs) for medico-legal and other clinical purposes: DSM-5-TR PTSD, ICD-11 PTSD and ICD-11 complex PTSD
evidence.snippet: the clinical presentation of PTSDs; psychological models of PTSDs; how to approach assessment and differential diagnosis

nodes.description: Recurrent intrusive traumatic memories, flashbacks, and nightmares.

evidence.reference_title: Epigenetic Alterations in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: Comprehensive Review of Molecular Markers
evidence.snippet: PTSD is characterized by nightmares, flashbacks and avoidance of stressors.

nodes.description: PTSD is associated with peripheral inflammatory markers and immune-system changes that may affect brain circuits involved in fear, anxiety, and emotion regulation.

evidence.reference_title: The role of the immune system in posttraumatic stress disorder.
evidence.snippet: some individuals with PTSD also exhibit elevated concentrations of inflammatory markers, including C-reactive protein, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-α.

evidence.reference_title: Inflammation in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD): A Review of Potential Correlates of PTSD with a Neurological Perspective.
evidence.snippet: Major findings included elevated levels of serum proinflammatory cytokines in individuals with PTSD across various trauma types, as compared with those without PTSD.

nodes.description: Common-variant genetic liability increases the probability of probable PTSD after traumatic injury, but does not by itself cause PTSD without a trauma context. PTSD genetic liability overlaps with genetic risk for other neurodegenerative conditions including REM sleep behavior disorder, suggesting shared genetic mechanisms underlying trauma response and neurodegenerative risk.
nodes.id: Polygenic Susceptibility to PTSD after Trauma

evidence.reference_title: Polygenic risk for mental disorders as predictors of posttraumatic stress disorder after mild traumatic brain injury
evidence.snippet: We used data from individuals of European ancestry with mTBI enrolled in TRACK-TBI (n = 714), a prospective longitudinal study of level 1 trauma center patients.

evidence.reference_title: Polygenic risk for mental disorders as predictors of posttraumatic stress disorder after mild traumatic brain injury
evidence.snippet: The adjusted odds of PTSD in the uppermost PTSD-PRS quintile was nearly four times higher (aOR = 3.71, 95% CI 1.80–7.65) than in the lowest PTSD-PRS quintile.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic associations between post-traumatic stress disorder and REM-sleep behavior disorder.
evidence.snippet: While not supporting a causal relationship, genetic analyses revealed a significant association between PTSD and iRBD, consistent with the exploratory imaging substudy. These findings suggest that individuals genetically at risk for PTSD may also be at higher risk for iRBD.

nodes.description: SSRI and SNRI pharmacotherapy can be used for PTSD symptom reduction, including sertraline, paroxetine, and venlafaxine.

evidence.reference_title: Pharmacological therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis of monotherapy, augmentation and head-to-head approaches.
evidence.snippet: For individual monotherapy agents compared to placebo in two or more studies, we found small statistically significant evidence for the antidepressants fluoxetine, paroxetine, sertraline, venlafaxine and the antipsychotic quetiapine.

nodes.description: Altered amygdala, hippocampal, and prefrontal cortical function affects threat detection, emotional regulation, fear reactivity, and traumatic memory processing.

evidence.reference_title: Inflammation in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD): A Review of Potential Correlates of PTSD with a Neurological Perspective.
evidence.snippet: particular brain regions may be identifiable as neural correlates of PTSD, including the prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and amygdala of the brain.

nodes.description: First-line trauma-focused psychotherapies for PTSD include exposure therapy such as prolonged exposure, cognitive processing therapy, trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy, EMDR, and narrative exposure therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Psychological treatments for adults with posttraumatic stress disorder: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: Evidence supports efficacy of exposure therapy (high SOE) including the manualized version Prolonged Exposure (PE); cognitive therapy (CT), cognitive processing therapy (CPT), cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)-mixed therapies (moderate SOE); eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR) and narrative exposure therapy (low-moderate SOE).

evidence.reference_title: Comparison of Prolonged Exposure vs Cognitive Processing Therapy for Treatment of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Among US Veterans: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
evidence.snippet: This randomized clinical trial found that although PE was statistically more effective than CPT, the difference was not clinically significant, and improvements in PTSD were meaningful in both treatment groups.

nodes.description: Exposure to psychologically threatening or horrific traumatic events is the initiating environmental context required for PTSD.

evidence.reference_title: Assessment and diagnosis of post-traumatic stress disorders (PTSDs) for medico-legal and other clinical purposes: DSM-5-TR PTSD, ICD-11 PTSD and ICD-11 complex PTSD
evidence.snippet: Given ongoing debate, the article proposes that trauma exposure is best defined in future iterations of the DSM and ICD as exposure to one or more psychologically threatening or horrific experiences that are overwhelming.
